sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 0.1g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, clean nutrient density is the real test.
sections.subtitleText: 0.1g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, clean nutrient density is the real test.

sections.title: Carbs
sections.narration: 9.3g of carbs. the sugar load matters more than you would want. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.
sections.subtitleText: 9.3g of carbs. the sugar load matters more than you would want. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: 1.1g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is not the main pitch here, but extra support still matters.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: pros first: very low downside whole food because vegetables win a lot simply by offering clean nutritional payoff. easy to build meals around because a practical plus because good foods need realistic use cases. good volume for the calories because that matters for satiety and general usefulness.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: the drawbacks next: energy density is very low because good for some use cases, but not enough on its own. usually needs pairing to make a full meal because most vegetables are not complete by themselves. freshness and prep can matter because practical friction is still a real downside.

closing.summary: Onion does have a few real-world upsides. but the nutrition case is still weak and the downsides drag it down hard. best only when you need a low-commitment add-on rather than a nutrient-dense anchor.
closing.useCaseNote: Best only when you need a low-commitment add-on rather than a nutrient-dense anchor.
closing.cta: Would you rank it the same, or nah?

explanation.summary: Fats are carrying this food most, while minerals are the biggest drag. It lands in C tier.
